title: Playwright Rendering, Isolation, Readiness, and Dynamic-DOM Evidence · Retrieval Circuit
summary: A delayed incident review reconstructs playwright rendering, isolation, readiness, and dynamic-dom evidence from revision, authorization, seed, URL, policy, response bytes, parsed identity, record provenance, frontier transition, Promise settlement, browser state, report row, failure, cleanup, and recovery evidence. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for isolated evidence-driven rendering adapter after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, chan…
objectives: Render only when static evidence cannot satisfy an authorized stakeholder requirement and retain the decision cause.
Create bounded incognito-like BrowserContexts with explicit storage, permission, download, service-worker, locale, viewport, and close policy.
Wait for application-specific stable evidence under time and request budgets instead of treating network idle as universal readiness.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Render Necessity · changed case · Review Render Necessity
steps.instruction:
review case — A review TypeScript team handles case 6382 during an authorized municipal service audit. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove necessity, context isolation, readiness, browser network policy, provenance, and closure; URL, robots, body, parser, frontier, privacy, accessibility, and report budgets stay explicit; then change one duplicate, accessibility state, axe result, resource budget, browser readiness, or manual-review condition without overclaiming. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. stimulus.caption: Three excerpts from a learner evidence packet; only one connects a claim to a reproducible check.

lines.label: assumption
lines.text: A review TypeScript team handles case 7096 during an authorized municipal service audit. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove necessity, context isolation, readiness, browser network policy, provenance, and closure; owner authorization and revocation must remain separate from robots and legal-review claims; then inject one authenticated, private-data, output-injection, path, log, plugin, or correction fault and contain it. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Bind browser version, context policy, viewport, actions, DOM digest, screenshot or trace identity, console errors, and closure evidence.

lines.label: risk
lines.text: Rendered HTML alone explains the browser state that produced it.

lines.label: evidence
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Bind browser version, context policy, viewport, actions, DOM digest, screenshot or trace identity, console errors, and closure evidence. Artifact under review: isolated evidence-driven rendering adapter.

options.text: The record names the assumption, applies Rendered Provenance, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.

The Rendered Provenance record says isolated evidence-driven rendering adapter looks right but supplies no governing condition, observation, changed case, or counterexample. That claim fails the separate evidence from confidence evidence boundary for Rendered Provenance.

For separate evidence from confidence, fetch one convenient page, omit owner authorization, source and emitted revision, URL and robots policy, runtime admission, stream and Promise ownership, Cheerio and browser differences, accessibility states, changed fixtures, recovery, and controlled Node transfer, then accept plausible objects as TypeScript crawler proof. This does not satisfy Rendered Provenance for isolated evidence-driven rendering adapter.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Rendered Provenance
steps.instruction:
Write the review evidence note for Rendered Provenance: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A review TypeScript team handles case 2953 during a hostile redirect, stream, and checkpoint drill. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove necessity, context isolation, readiness, browser network policy, provenance, and closure; release evidence binds source, compiler, emitted JavaScript, lockfile, package, browser revision, container, configuration, and restored state; then reorder one frontier, p-limit, Promise, AbortSignal, stream, retry, checkpoint, or shutdown event and prove ownership. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Bind browser version, context policy, viewport, actions, DOM digest, screenshot or trace identity, console errors, and closure evidence.

Evidence target: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Rendered Provenance.
steps.why: Retrieving Rendered Provenance through the concrete isolated evidence-driven rendering adapter case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Rendered Provenance, keep Rendered Provenance connected to executable TypeScript authorization, URL, runtime admission, Fetch, Promise, browser, accessibility, report, package, and recovery decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Bind browser version, context policy, viewport, actions, DOM digest, screenshot or trace identity, console errors, and closure evidence.

hints: For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Rendered Provenance, name the owner, authorization, source and emitted revision, Node, TypeScript, URL, robots, unknown value, Fetch, Promise, AbortSignal, browser, accessibility, report, handle, recovery, and native-transfer boundary, Rendered Provenance precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Rendered Provenance, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A review TypeScript team handles case 2953 during a hostile redirect, stream, and checkpoint drill. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove necessity, context isolation, readiness, browser network policy, provenance, and closure; release evidence binds source, compiler, emitted JavaScript, lockfile, package, browser revision, container, configuration, and restored state; then reorder one frontier, p-limit, Promise, AbortSignal, stream, retry, checkpoint, or shutdown event and prove ownership. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Bind browser version, context policy, viewport, actions, DOM digest, screenshot or trace identity, console errors, and closure evidence.

For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Rendered Provenance, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: Rendered HTML alone explains the browser state that produced it.
